title: Floquet second-order topological insulator in strained graphene
authors: Yu-Wen Xu, Xiaolin Wan, Zi-Ming Wang, Rui Wang, Dong-Hui Xu
abstract: Graphene provides a canonical setting for Floquet band engineering, where circularly polarized light can dynamically open topological gaps at Dirac points and generate nonequilibrium Hall responses. Here we show that uniaxial strain and off-resonant circularly polarized light with tunable incidence angle enable a controllable route to Floquet higher-order topology in graphene. Using a strained honeycomb tight-binding model with Peierls coupling and a high-frequency expansion for the effective Floquet Hamiltonian, we find that strain drives the Dirac cones toward the Dirac-merging (semi-Dirac) critical regime, where the light-induced mass becomes strongly anisotropic. For oblique incidence, the projected drive is effectively elliptically polarized and, in combination with strain, stabilizes a phase with gapped edges but robust in-gap corner modes in finite geometries, realizing a Floquet second-order topological insulator. We characterize the phase diagram via the Chern number and a crystalline-symmetry-quantized polarization invariant. Finally, first-principles-informed tight-binding calculations corroborate the predicted topological evolution in strained graphene nanostructures. Our results identify driven strained graphene as a realistic and tunable platform for realizing and diagnosing Floquet higher-order topological phases.
summary: This paper shows that by applying both strain and oblique circularly polarized light to graphene, one can create a Floquet second-order topological insulator. This phase is unique because it features protected corner modes despite having gapped edges. The study provides a tunable platform for exploring higher-order topological physics in a realistic material.
main_problem: The study aims to find a controllable way to realize Floquet higher-order topological insulator phases in graphene using light and strain.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that combining uniaxial strain with off-resonant circularly polarized light at oblique incidence stabilizes a Floquet second-order topological insulator phase characterized by gapped edges and robust corner modes.
method: The researchers use a strained honeycomb tight-binding model with Peierls coupling and a high-frequency expansion for the effective Floquet Hamiltonian, supplemented by first-principles-informed tight-binding calculations.
model_or_system: The system is graphene under uniaxial strain, subjected to off-resonant circularly polarized light with a tunable incidence angle.
key_observables: Chern number and a crystalline-symmetry-quantized polarization invariant.
important_parameters: Uniaxial strain, light incidence angle (oblique incidence), and the transition to the Dirac-merging (semi-Dirac) critical regime.
main_assumptions: The study utilizes a high-frequency expansion for the effective Floquet Hamiltonian, implying the driving frequency is much larger than the relevant energy scales.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of Floquet band engineering in graphene, presents a theoretical model involving strain and light, analyzes the resulting topological phases and invariants, and validates the findings with first-principles-informed calculations.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
